Title: Active deep neural network features selection for segmentation and recognition of brain tumors using MRI images
Architecture_Specifics: Saliency-based deep learning (SbDL) using AlexNet and Inception V3 with DRLBP features and PSO-based feature selection
Dataset_Size: BRATS 2013: 30 patients (20 HGG, 10 LGG); BRATS 2015: 274 cases (220 HGG, 54 LGG); BRATS 2017: 431 cases (210 HGG, 75 LGG, 146 testing); BRATS 2018: 476 cases (210 HGG, 75 LGG, 191 testing)
Training_Data_Source: Public BRATS series datasets (2013, 2015, 2017, 2018) of HGG and LGG brain tumor MRI
Other_Metrics: Dice similarity (DS) rate, error rate, classification accuracy, execution time, F1 Score (from cited work), LOPO validation (from cited work)
Performance_Summary: For segmentation, the method achieved Dice scores on BRATS2017 of 83.73% (core tumor), 93.7% (whole tumor), and 79.94% (enhanced tumor), and on BRATS2018 of 88.34% (core), 91.2% (whole), and 81.84% (enhanced), with average DS of 95.422% (error 4.578%) on a 100-patient BRATS2017 subset and 93.238% (error 6.7%) on a 200-sample BRATS2018 subset. For classification, softmax with selected features achieved accuracies of 98.3% (BRATS2013), 97.8% (BRATS2015), 96.9% (BRATS2017), and 92.5% (BRATS2018), outperforming MSVM, KNN, and ensemble classifiers.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The work uses standard BRATS benchmark datasets and GPU-based deep learning (AlexNet, Inception V3, PSO), with no discussion of low-field MRI, cost, or deployment in resource-limited settings, though automated segmentation/classification could be broadly useful.
Main_Finding_1: The proposed SbDL saliency-based segmentation method, combined with PIaL contrast enhancement, achieves high Dice scores for core, whole, and enhanced tumor regions on BRATS2017 and BRATS2018, outperforming prior methods such as Wang et al. and Isensee et al. on these benchmarks.
Main_Finding_2: Fusion of Inception V3 deep features with DRLBP texture features followed by PSO-based feature selection and softmax classification yields very high brain tumor modality classification accuracies (>92% across BRATS 2013, 2015, 2017, and 2018), with best accuracy of 98.3% on BRATS2013.
Main_Finding_3: The PSO-based active feature selection step both improves classification accuracy compared to using raw or fused features and reduces execution time, mitigating the computational overhead introduced by feature fusion.
Limitations_Mentioned: The paper notes that fusion of DRLBP and CNN features increases computational time, and that patch-based CNN models can lose core information and be time-consuming; it also acknowledges performance variation across datasets and suggests future work using Capsule Net to further improve accuracy. No explicit discussion of generalization to non-BRATS clinical data or scanner variability is provided.
Notes_Questions: The study focuses on segmentation and classification of brain tumor MRI using BRATS datasets and does not involve super-resolution despite the systematic review context. No PSNR/SSIM or image quality metrics are reported, and there is no external clinical validation beyond BRATS benchmarks.

Title: Brain tumor segmentation in MRI with multi-modality spatial information enhancement and boundary shape correction
Architecture_Specifics: CNN-based 3D brain tumor segmentation network with U-Net-like encoder-decoder backbone enhanced by Modality Information Extraction (MIE), Spatial Information Enhancement (SIE), and Boundary Shape Correction (BSC) modules
Dataset_Size: BraTS2017, 2018, and 2019 datasets
Training_Data_Source: Public BraTS2017, BraTS2018, and BraTS2019 multimodal brain tumor MRI datasets
Other_Metrics: Not reported
Performance_Summary: The proposed method was validated on the BraTS2017, 2018, and 2019 datasets and achieved state-of-the-art brain tumor segmentation performance compared with existing methods. An ablation study demonstrated that each of the three modules (MIE, SIE, BSC) contributed to performance improvements.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The work uses standard high-resource GPU infrastructure (8×P100) and public BraTS datasets without discussion of low-field MRI, deployment constraints, or LMIC-specific scenarios, giving it limited direct relevance to LMIC settings.
Main_Finding_1: A multimodal spatial information enhancement and boundary shape correction framework with MIE, SIE, and BSC modules achieves state-of-the-art brain tumor segmentation performance on BraTS2017, 2018, and 2019 datasets.
Main_Finding_2: The MIE module uses an attention mechanism to weight and fuse multimodal MRI information, improving utilization of modality-specific features for segmentation.
Main_Finding_3: The SIE module expands the spatial receptive field via multi-scale dilated convolutions and the BSC module introduces a spatial critical boundary point selection algorithm with a boundary correction loss to enhance boundary segmentation accuracy.
Limitations_Mentioned: The paper notes general challenges such as heterogeneity of brain tumors, loss of spatial information due to pooling and upsampling, and underutilization of boundary information in existing methods, but specific limitations of the proposed method (e.g., generalization, computational cost, or clinical validation gaps) are not explicitly detailed in the provided text.
Notes_Questions: This is a segmentation-focused paper, not an MRI super-resolution study, despite being included in an SR-focused review. No PSNR/SSIM or image quality metrics are reported; evaluation appears to be via standard segmentation metrics that are not shown in the excerpt. Full paper is needed for exact dataset sizes and quantitative metrics (e.g., Dice, Hausdorff).

Title: Deep-learning approach with convolutional neural network for classification of maximum intensity projections of dynamic contrast-enhanced breast magnetic resonance imaging
Architecture_Specifics: DenseNet121, DenseNet161, InceptionResNetV2, InceptionV3, NASNetMobile, Xception
Dataset_Size: For the training and validation phase, we used a total set of 286 images (31 normal, 75 benign, and 180 malignant cases). For the test phase, a total of 72 images (12 normal, 13 benign, and 47 malignant cases) were used.
Training_Data_Source: Retrospective DCE breast MRI maximum intensity projections from a single hospital (Tokyo Medical and Dental University) acquired between January 2014 and December 2018.
Other_Metrics: Sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC)
Performance_Summary: Across six CNN models, the mean AUC was 0.830 (range, 0.750–0.895). The best model, InceptionResNetV2, achieved sensitivity of 74.5%, specificity of 96.0%, and AUC of 0.895 on the test set. Human readers achieved AUCs of 0.832 and 0.849 with sensitivities of 72.3% and 78.7% and specificities of 88.0% and 80.0%, respectively, with no statistically significant differences compared to the CNNs (p > 0.125).
Clinical_Results: Two human readers (a breast surgeon and an experienced breast radiologist) independently interpreted the same 72 MIP test images using BI-RADS categories. Their diagnostic performance (AUC 0.832 and 0.849) was comparable to that of the best CNN model (AUC 0.895), with no significant differences in ROC analysis (p > 0.125). Both readers and CNNs shared similar patterns of false positives and false negatives, particularly for small or weakly enhancing lesions.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The work uses standard 3.0 T MRI and a GPU workstation, which may be resource-intensive for some LMIC settings, but it targets workflow efficiency (abbreviated MRI protocols and automated MIP classification) and radiologist shortage, issues that are also relevant in LMICs.
Main_Finding_1: CNN models trained on MIP images of DCE breast MRI achieved diagnostic performance (mean AUC 0.83, best AUC 0.895) comparable to human readers for differentiating benign from malignant lesions.
Main_Finding_2: The best-performing architecture, InceptionResNetV2, reached 74.5% sensitivity and 96.0% specificity, slightly outperforming human readers in specificity without statistically significant differences.
Main_Finding_3: Misclassified cases by CNNs were often small or weakly enhancing malignant lesions or strongly enhancing benign lesions, and these were also frequently misclassified by human readers, suggesting shared diagnostic challenges.
Limitations_Mentioned: The authors note several limitations: (1) relatively small, single-center dataset that may not adequately represent both rare and common diseases, limiting generalizability and making clinical deployment premature; (2) some lesions were diagnosed by follow-up rather than histology; (3) images were converted from DICOM to 512×512 JPEG due to software constraints, potentially losing contrast and gradation information; (4) diagnostic performance of readers and CNNs was compared using different scales (BI-RADS 1–5 vs. 0–100% malignancy probability), which may affect statistical comparisons and requires careful determination of cut-off values; (5) the CNN probability outputs depend on the specific training data, necessitating training and validation in diverse cohorts; and (6) use of DCE fat-suppressed T1-weighted axial imaging means poor fat suppression could mimic enhancement and affect model performance, and future work should explore 3D thin-slice data and object detection.
Notes_Questions: This is a classification study on MIP images of DCE breast MRI, not a super-resolution task, so no image quality metrics like PSNR/SSIM are reported. The study uses transfer learning from ImageNet and multiple modern CNN architectures, with 500-epoch training and basic augmentation. Dataset size is modest and from a single 3T scanner, which may limit robustness; no external validation is described.

Title: Improvement of peripheral nerve visualization using a deep learning-based MR reconstruction algorithm
Architecture_Specifics: deep learning based AIR Recon DL (DLRecon), a deep convolution neural network
Dataset_Size: 29 subjects, 60 peripheral nerves, 60 axial 2D intermediate-weighted FSE sequences
Training_Data_Source: Previously trained on pairs of conventional MR images and ‘near-perfect’ images using four million unique image pairs from diverse anatomies, contrasts, and coil configurations
Other_Metrics: Odds ratios (OR) with 95% confidence intervals and p-values; ordinal-weighted Gwet's agreement coefficients (AC) with clustered bootstrap confidence intervals; distribution of Likert scores for image quality and artifact grades; percentage of correct identification of SOC vs DLRecon images
Performance_Summary: DLRecon-MRIs were more likely to score higher than SOC-MRIs for outer epineurium conspicuity (Reader 2 OR 1.9, p=0.007) and visualization of fascicular architecture (Reader 2 OR 1.8, p<0.001). DLRecon-MRIs were more likely to score worse for ghosting (Reader 2 OR 2.8, p<0.001; Reader 1 OR 1.7, p=0.001) and pulsation artifacts (Reader 2 OR 1.6, p=0.004). Inter-reconstruction agreement between SOC and DLRecon was substantial to almost-perfect for all features (AC 0.73–1.00).
Clinical_Results: Two radiologists, blinded to reconstruction type, independently graded 60 peripheral nerve MRIs for outer epineurium conspicuity, fascicular architecture, and artifacts. DLRecon significantly improved outer epineurium conspicuity and fascicular architecture visualization for one reader and showed a similar trend for the other, while increasing ghosting and pulsation artifacts that did not impede nerve assessment. Interrater agreement for outer epineurium conspicuity improved from fair with SOC (AC 0.34) to substantial with DLRecon (AC 0.71), and readers correctly identified SOC vs DLRecon images in 83–91% of cases, indicating noticeable but clinically acceptable texture differences.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The work is based on a 3.0T GE MR750 scanner with a vendor-provided proprietary DL reconstruction (DLRecon), which limits applicability in low-resource or non-GE settings. While the method could in principle trade improved reconstruction for shorter scans or higher resolution, the study does not address low-field systems, cost, or deployment in constrained environments.
Main_Finding_1: DLRecon significantly improved conspicuity of the outer epineurium and visualization of fascicular architecture—two key morphological features for peripheral nerve injury assessment—compared to standard-of-care reconstruction.
Main_Finding_2: DLRecon increased the severity of ghosting and pulsation artifacts, but these artifacts generally did not interfere with nerve conspicuity or overall image interpretation.
Main_Finding_3: Inter-reconstruction agreement between SOC and DLRecon was substantial to almost-perfect for all evaluated features, and DLRecon improved interrater agreement for outer epineurium conspicuity from fair to substantial.
Limitations_Mentioned: The authors note a moderate sample size and heterogeneous anatomy and imaging parameters, which increased variability in acquired data. Only a single contrast (2D intermediate-weighted FSE) was evaluated, whereas heavily T2-weighted fat-suppressed sequences commonly used in MR neurography were not assessed and are currently incompatible with DLRecon. Readers scored entire image stacks rather than single slices, potentially increasing scoring variability but reflecting clinical practice. DLRecon is a vendor-provided GE product, limiting use to that vendor’s scanners, and the study did not evaluate its impact on scan time or spatial resolution trade-offs.
Notes_Questions: This is not a pure super-resolution study; DLRecon performs denoising and Gibbs ringing removal, effectively enhancing edge sharpness and apparent resolution without changing k-space sampling. No standard image quality metrics such as PSNR or SSIM are reported; evaluation is entirely reader-based with ordinal statistics. Training details (4 million image pairs, diverse anatomies) are referenced from prior work but the exact training dataset composition is not specified.

Title: Enhancement of brain tumor classification from MRI images using multi-path convolutional neural network with SVM classifier
Architecture_Specifics: Multi-path convolutional neural network (CNN) with Multi-class Support Vector Machine (MSVM) classifier; dual-path CNN-SVM based on simplified single-path CNNs from Yazdan et al. [29]
Dataset_Size: Not reported
Training_Data_Source: Two MRI datasets of brain images containing four classes (glioma, meningioma, pituitary, and healthy), combined and also used separately; specific dataset names and sources not reported in the provided text
Other_Metrics: Recall 99.5 %, Specificity 98.9 %, Precision 97.3 %, F1 score 98.4 %, AUC, MCC, Kappa, number of parameters, training time (values for the latter metrics not given in the excerpt)
Performance_Summary: The proposed dual-path CNN with SVM classifier achieved an accuracy of 98.3 % on the First dataset, 98.2 % on the Second dataset, and 99.1 % on the combination of both datasets for four-class brain tumor classification (glioma, meningioma, pituitary, healthy). Reported recall, specificity, precision, and F1 score on the combined dataset were 99.5 %, 98.9 %, 97.3 %, and 98.4 %, respectively. The method is claimed to improve accuracy by more than 10 % over prior work while reducing the number of trainable parameters.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The work explicitly aims to reduce network complexity and training parameters, which can benefit resource-constrained settings, but it does not address low-field MRI, hardware limitations, or LMIC-specific deployment directly.
Main_Finding_1: A dual-path CNN architecture with an SVM classifier achieved up to 99.1 % accuracy for four-class brain tumor classification on combined MRI datasets, outperforming earlier works by more than 10 % in accuracy according to the authors.
Main_Finding_2: Replacing the conventional SoftMax classifier with a Multi-class SVM layer produced the greatest improvement in accuracy and sensitivity, positively influencing sensitivity, specificity, and precision.
Main_Finding_3: The proposed CNN-SVM model uses significantly fewer training parameters than prior architectures while maintaining or improving classification performance, indicating reduced model complexity.
Limitations_Mentioned: Not reported in the provided text excerpt.
Notes_Questions: The paper focuses on classification rather than image super-resolution, despite the systematic review’s SR scope. Exact dataset sizes, field strengths, and whether the datasets are public (e.g., standard brain tumor benchmarks) are not specified in the excerpt; full text is needed to clarify dataset details and any stated limitations.

Title: A deep learning approach for brain tumor classification using MRI images
Architecture_Specifics: Pre-trained EfficientNet and ResNet50 feature extractors with PLS-based feature fusion, proposal generation via agglomerative clustering and structural edge detector, ROI pooling, deconvolution layers, and final SoftMax classifier
Dataset_Size: 233 patients, 3064 T1-weighted enhanced contrast brain MRI scans
Training_Data_Source: Public Figshare brain tumor dataset by Cheng et al., collected from 233 patients at two state-owned hospitals in Guangzhou and Tianjin, China between 2005 and 2010
Other_Metrics: Accuracy, specificity, sensitivity (recall), precision, f1-score
Performance_Summary: Using 5-fold cross-validation with 70% training and 30% validation, the proposed method achieved an overall brain tumor classification accuracy of 98.95% on the Figshare dataset. The confusion matrix shows 3009 correctly classified and 55 misclassified cases, with particularly strong performance on Glioma due to augmented training data.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The work targets automated brain tumor classification to reduce radiologist workload and mentions medical resource imbalance, but it does not specifically address low-field MRI or deployment in low-resource clinical settings.
Main_Finding_1: The proposed deep learning-based brain tumor classification method achieved a high overall accuracy of 98.95% on the public Figshare T1-weighted contrast-enhanced MRI dataset using 5-fold cross-validation.
Main_Finding_2: Combining features from two pre-trained networks (EfficientNet and ResNet50) via PLS-based fusion and using proposal generation with agglomerative clustering and ROI pooling substantially improved classification performance compared to prior methods.
Main_Finding_3: Custom image enhancement (illumination boost with logarithmic and exponential transforms plus CDF-HSD and non-linear stretching) and extensive data augmentation (rotations and flipping, tripling the dataset to 9192 images) improved input image quality and reduced overfitting.
Limitations_Mentioned: The paper notes general challenges such as poor image quality, lack of training data, low-quality features, and poor tumor localization in existing methods, but does not provide a dedicated limitations section or detailed discussion of limitations of the proposed model (e.g., single public dataset, lack of external or clinical validation).
Notes_Questions: The study focuses on classification of three tumor types (Pituitary, Meningioma, Glioma) from T1-weighted contrast-enhanced MRI and does not involve super-resolution despite the systematic review’s focus; no PSNR/SSIM or image reconstruction metrics are reported. Field strength and scanner details are not specified beyond being T1-weighted enhanced images from two Chinese hospitals.

Title: Automatic deep learning-based myocardial infarction segmentation from delayed enhancement MRI
Architecture_Specifics: CNN- UNET
Dataset_Size: 195 DE-MRI examinations , 1323 DE-MRI slices, 904 slices with infarcted areas
Training_Data_Source: Single center data from the University Hospital of Dijon, France
Other_Metrics: Dice index and Jaccard index, PIM difference
Performance_Summary: ‎The proposed deep learning framework achieved a Dice coefficient of 0.668 (Jaccard 0.524) for fully automatic infarction segmentation and 0.843 (jaccard 0.746) when the myocardium mask was given.For middle slices only, Dice improved to 0.694 (unknown mask) and 0.845 (known mask)
Clinical_Results: Results reveal that the proposal has a very similar performance to the inter observer variation study and results from the GMM are significantly inferior
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The method relies on standard high-field 1.5T and 3T MRI scanners and requires a relatively large, manually annotated dataset with GPU-based deep learning training
Main_Finding_1: The deep learning approach really outperformed the GMM method demonstrating superiority of CNN based segmentation
Main_Finding_2: The proposed two stage CNN framework archived strong myocardial infarction segmentation performance reaching a Dice score of 0.668( fully automatic)and 0.843when the myocardial mask was provided with results comparable to inter observer expert variability.
Limitations_Mentioned: The authors acknowledges that segmentation performance decreases in basal and apical slices, which are more difficult and uncertain to delineate. The model struggles in cases with large no-reflow regions, where no-reflow tissue may be confused with normal myocardium.

Title: Deep learning methods for automatic evaluation of delayed enhancement-MRI. The results of the EMIDEC challenge
Architecture_Specifics: Two conventional U-Net variants for segmentation; CNN-based classifiers for distinguishing infarct vs non-infarct exams; some methods incorporated complex pipelines with additional parameters
Dataset_Size: 150 exams (50 non-infarct, 100 with myocardial infarction)
Training_Data_Source: EMIDEC dataset from University Hospital of Dijon, France; short-axis DE-MRI with associated clinical characteristics
Other_Metrics: Dice score (myocardium and infarction area), classification accuracy
Performance_Summary: Best segmentation method achieved Dice of 0.879 for myocardium and 0.712 for infarction area; best classification method reached accuracy of 0.92
Clinical_Results: Automatic classification between infarct and non-infarct exams is feasible; myocardium segmentation is reliable; scar and PMO segmentation remains challenging
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: No discussion of low-field MRI, deployment costs, or resource-limited settings; uses standard hospital-acquired DE-MRI with contrast agents, which requires infrastructure not always available in LMICs
Main_Finding_1: Automatic classification of DE-MRI exams as infarct or non-infarct is achievable with deep learning, with a best accuracy of 0.92 using clinical data alongside imaging
Main_Finding_2: U-Net based methods provide accurate myocardium delineation with a best Dice score of 0.879, demonstrating reliable automated segmentation of the left ventricular wall
Main_Finding_3: Segmentation of the diseased area (infarction and PMO) remains difficult due to small lesion sizes and low contrast with surrounding structures, with best Dice of 0.712
Limitations_Mentioned: Small diseased area size, lack of contrast between scar tissue and surrounding structures, complex pipelines did not consistently outperform simpler ones, PMO segmentation needs further improvement
Notes_Questions: Paper presents a challenge overview aggregating multiple methods rather than a single model; clinical data were included alongside imaging, which is a distinguishing feature; no PSNR or SSIM reported as this is a segmentation and classification task, not super-resolution

Title: Deep learning-based super-resolution gradient echo imaging of the pancreas: Improvement of image quality and reduction of acquisition time
Architecture_Specifics: sub-pixel CNN
Dataset_Size: 50 MRI examinations, 80 Slices
Training_Data_Source: The data for this retrospective, single-center (monocentric) study was obtained from patients who underwent clinically indicated MRI examinations at the Eberhard Karls University Tuebingen in Germany
Other_Metrics: Not reported
Performance_Summary: Significantly reduced mean acquisition time to 13 ± 1 s compared to 15 ± 1 s for standard imaging (P < 0.001). Qualitative analysis showed that VIBE SR significantly outperformed standard VIBE in all categories. Quantitative lesion size measurementsremained consistent between both methods, demonstrating no significant diagnostic difference (P > 0.05)
Clinical_Results: Two radiologists rated the super-resolution images significantly better than standard images across all qualitative categories, including noise, sharpness, diagnostic confidence, and lesion detectability (all p < 0.001), while quantitative lesion size measurements remained consistent (p > 0.05). Inter-reader agreement was moderate for qualitative image quality scores (kappa: 0.53–0.56) and excellent for lesion size measurements (ICC: 0.994–0.998)
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The method is a software-based post-processing solution that runs on standard CPUs with fast inference times (15 seconds), requiring no specialized hardware or modifications to existing clinical MRI sequences.
Main_Finding_1: The algorithm significantly improved qualitative image quality
Main_Finding_2: The implementation of aggressive partial Fourier sampling within the super-resolution pipeline enabled a significant reduction in mean acquisition time from 15 seconds to 13 seconds.
Main_Finding_3: Clinical validation showed that lesion detectability was significantly enhanced, while quantitative lesion size measurements remained consistent with standard imaging (p > 0.05), ensuring the technique's diagnostic reliability
Limitations_Mentioned: No experience using the model at higher or lower field strengths, small dataset, retrospective study design, lack of quantitative metrics

Title: Super-resolution and denoising of 4D-Flow MRI using physics-Informed deep neural nets
Architecture_Specifics: Physics-Informed Deep Neural Net (PI-DNN) with 10 hidden layers and 25-30 neurons per layer
Dataset_Size: Not reported
Training_Data_Source: Synthetic data from CFD simulations of an idealized intracranial aneurysm and a cylinder wake. In-vitro data from a silicone phantom model scanned with a 7T MRI system and validated with PIV.
Other_Metrics: vNRMSE, sNRMSE, ASI, MSI and SI.
Performance_Summary: On synthetic data, the method increased spatial resolution by 100x and temporal resolution by 5x, achieving an order of magnitude reduction in vNRMSE. For in-vitro data with PIV as reference, vNRMSE was reduced by 50%, and the DNN prediction qualitatively matched PIV, though a systematic bias from the 4D-Flow MRI measurement remained.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The method reduces the need for manual expertise in segmentation and CFD setup, which is a barrier in low-resource settings. However, it was developed and tested on 7T MRI data, which is not accessible in LMICs. The technique is transferable in principle, but its performance on low-field systems is untested.
Main_Finding_1: The proposed physics-informed DNN method can successfully perform super-resolution and denoising on 4D-Flow MRI data without requiring accurate geometry or boundary conditions, a major advantage over traditional CFD-based data assimilation.
Main_Finding_2: On synthetic data with a known ground truth, the method achieved significant improvements (100x spatial, 5x temporal resolution) and eliminated velocity aliasing artifacts by working with complex Cartesian image data rather than phase images.
Main_Finding_3: In validation against high-resolution in-vitro PIV data, the method produced qualitatively superior flow fields compared to raw 4D-Flow MRI. However, quantitative error metrics (vNRMSE) showed only a 50% improvement, indicating a persistent systematic bias from the original MRI measurement that the model could not correct.
Limitations_Mentioned: The in-vitro validation showed no significant improvement in gross quantitative similarity indices (ASI, MSI, SI) over the original 4D-Flow MRI. The method's assumptions about spatio-temporal averaging may not perfectly match actual k-space acquisition. Hyperparameter tuning (e.g., loss function weights) is currently done in an ad-hoc manner.
Notes_Questions: This is a methodology paper, not a clinical application paper. It uses high-field (7T) data for validation.

Title: Multi-contrast high-field quality image synthesis for portable low-field MRI using generative adversarial networks and paired data
Architecture_Specifics: LowGAN (Stage 1: three 2D pix2pix networks; Stage 2: 3D patch-based U-Net)
Dataset_Size: 49 MS patients (37 training, 12 testing)
Training_Data_Source: Hospital of the University of Pennsylvania and NIH Clinical Center/ Paired 64mT (Hyperfine SWOOP) and 3T (Siemens) clinical scans from Penn and NIH
Other_Metrics: Least Cohen’s d=0.51, Highest Cohen’s d=2.37/ NCC (Normalized Cross-Correlation), Absolute Volume (mm³), Pearson's correlation coefficient
Performance_Summary: Overall, the NCC analysis revealed significantly higher correlation to the paired high-field images for the LowGAN stage 2 outputs in comparison to the input low-field images across T1w, FLAIR and T2w modalities, with the highest improvement in FLAIR, followed by T1w, and then T2w images. These findings suggest that the synthetic images were not only visually superior but also quantitatively more similar to high-field scans. This study did not artificially generate or obscure white-matter lesions present in the original low-field inputs, preserved total white matter lesion volume, and captured a known inverse relationship between lesion burden and thalamic volume.
Clinical_Results: Synthetic outputs did not artificially generate or obscure white-matter lesions present in the original low-field inputs, preserved total white matter lesion volume, and captured a known inverse relationship between lesion burden and thalamic volume.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: This paper develops SR for 64 mT portable low field MRI systems that have the potential to increase access to neuroimaging.
Main_Finding_1: LowGAN produced high-field like images without banding artifacts which can be successfully utilized by neuroimaging pipelines.
Main_Finding_2: LowGAN recovers clinically relevant volumes. The paper compared SynthSeg segmentations generated from LowGAN inputs to those generated from SynthSR inputs and highfield inputs by quantifying total volume in subcortical structures, cortical gray matter, lateral ventricles and white matter.
Main_Finding_3: LowGAN preserves white matter lesion burden and its relationship with thalamic volume.
Limitations_Mentioned: Specific focus on MS patients. Limited sample size.
Notes_Questions: This study is capable of producing LowGAN images that maintain clinically relevant volumes. Also, for MS, this study assures that portable low field MRI could play a role in point-of-care diagnosis, screening, triage, or follow-up in cases where typical, particularly periventricular, white matter lesions of sufficient size are encountered.

Title: Super-resolution of brain MRI images based on denoising diffusion probabilistic model
Architecture_Specifics: DDPM with optimized U-Net
Dataset_Size: 928 healthy adult subjects (27,840 T1 slices, 18,360 diffusion slices)
Training_Data_Source: Amsterdam Open MRI collection
Other_Metrics: LPIPS: 0.031
Performance_Summary: The proposed DDPM achieved the lowest LPIPS score (0.031) compared to VDSR (0.241) and SRGAN (0.062), indicating superior perceptual quality. While PSNR and SSIM were slightly lower than SRGAN, the model preserved clearer texture and structural details.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The method is computationally intensive ("time consume of inferencing is much longer" due to iterative denoising) and requires high-end GPUs (RTX 3060), making it less suitable for low-resource settings.
Main_Finding_1: DDPM generated super-resolution images with the lowest LPIPS scores, balancing sharpness and naturalness better than CNN or GAN-based methods.
Main_Finding_2: Incorporating self-attention mechanisms at multiple resolutions (32x32, 16x16, 8x8) improved image quality compared to using it only at 16x16.
Main_Finding_3: Traditional metrics like PSNR and SSIM diverged from visual quality at large magnification factors (8x), whereas LPIPS aligned better with human perception.
Limitations_Mentioned: Sampling speed is significantly slower than GAN and CNN methods due to the iterative denoising process; high computational requirements for 3D processing.
Notes_Questions: Field strength not explicitly stated in text, likely 3T given the dataset source.

Title: Fine-tuning deep learning model parameters for improved super-resolution of dynamic MRI with prior-knowledge
Architecture_Specifics: 3D U-Net with perceptual loss
Dataset_Size: 40 subjects (training), 3 subjects (testing)
Training_Data_Source: CHAOS challenge dataset and single-center volunteer data
Other_Metrics: SD of difference images
Performance_Summary: Fine-tuning the model with a subject-specific static prior scan significantly improved SSIM (from 0.939 to 0.957 for 6.25% k-space) and PSNR compared to the pre-trained model alone.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The method enables 16x acceleration in acquisition time, potentially increasing throughput. However, it requires a separate high-res prior scan and subject-specific fine-tuning (computationally demanding).
Main_Finding_1: Incorporating a subject-specific high-resolution static scan as prior knowledge for fine-tuning significantly improves dynamic MRI super-resolution.
Main_Finding_2: The method achieves high structural similarity (SSIM > 0.95) even with extremely low-resolution inputs (6.25% of k-space), allowing for theoretical 16x acquisition acceleration.
Main_Finding_3: Perceptual loss using a pre-trained medical U-Net was effective for training the SR model.
Limitations_Mentioned: Fine-tuning is slow (approx. 8 hours per subject); tested on healthy volunteers without interventional devices; U-Net architecture requires input interpolation.
Notes_Questions: Focuses on dynamic (4D) MRI.

Title: 3D-MRI super-resolution reconstruction using multi-modality based on multi-resolution CNN
Architecture_Specifics: MsFF-Net (Multi-scale Feature Fusion reconstruction Network)
Dataset_Size: 20 cases (NAMIC), 10 cases (IXI)
Training_Data_Source: NAMIC dataset and IXI dataset
Other_Metrics: Computational cost (FLOPs, parameters)
Performance_Summary: MsFF-Net achieved PSNR of 41.64 dB and SSIM of 0.9895 on the IXI dataset, significantly outperforming comparison methods (SRCNN3D, ReCNN). It effectively fused high-frequency priors from T1w images to super-resolve T2w images.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The model is explicitly described as having a "lightweight structure" with fewer parameters (0.71M) and lower computational cost (9.34G FLOPs) than state-of-the-art alternatives, making it suitable for resource-constrained environments.
Main_Finding_1: The proposed multi-scale feature fusion network (MsFF-Net) outperforms state-of-the-art methods in reconstruction quality while using significantly fewer parameters.
Main_Finding_2: A low-frequency information filtering (LFIF) module effectively suppresses interference from the guidance modality (T1), improving the extraction of high-frequency details.
Main_Finding_3: The model demonstrated strong generalization when applied to an unlearned dataset (IXI) acquired with different scanners and field strengths.
Limitations_Mentioned: The framework relies on a two-scale analysis and was not tested on additional scales; experiments were limited to a 2x scaling factor.

Title: Super-resolution reconstruction of knee magnetic resonance imaging based on deep learning
Architecture_Specifics: SRCNN-style hidden layers + ESPCN-style sub-pixel convolution output layer
Dataset_Size: Training: RFL second training set (291 natural images) — Natural Domain (Non Medical dataset). Testing: Set5 (5 natural images). Set14 (14 natural images). BSD300 (100 natural images from the Berkeley dataset) Knee MRI application: 23 sagittal knee MR volumes (512 × 512 × 23 slices), used to qualitatively demonstrate improved cartilage visualization for osteoarthritis-related imaging.
Training_Data_Source: I Do Imaging (IDI) dataset https://idoimaging.com/home
Other_Metrics: Runtime: 0.236 s MSE is defined, but is not reported
Performance_Summary: On standard Super Resolution benchmarks (scale ×3), the method reports PSNR 36.220 dB and runtime 0.236 s, outperforming bicubic (32.898 dB), SCN (33.914 dB), SRCNN (33.680 dB), and ESPCN (35.131 dB).
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The method is old, no clinical evaluation, model trained and tested on general domain images
Main_Finding_1: EMISR improves SR quality over classic and CNN baselines
Main_Finding_2: A cascaded “smart-network” (1×1 + 3×3 + 1×3 + 3×1) is used to approximate the receptive field benefits of larger kernels while reducing computational complexity/parameters
Main_Finding_3: The method is reported to better preserve high-frequency detail in osteoarticular cartilage regions than bicubic, SCN, SRCNN, and ESPCN
Limitations_Mentioned: Authors state there remains “a gap” between reconstructed edges and the original HR image; the method is relativley outpdated for current research
Notes_Questions: Outdated SR baseline. Evidence is weak: trained on natural images and only 23 knee MR volumes, with no SSIM or clinical validation, so clinical usefulness is unproven.

Title: Deep learning super-resolution reconstruction for fast and high-quality cine cardiovascular magnetic resonance
Architecture_Specifics: Vendor multi-stage CNN reconstruction pipeline: consist of: - Adaptive-CS-Net for compressed sensing reconstruction - followed by Precise Image Net (CNN-based post-processing) for denoising, de-aliasing, artifact removal, and resolution upscaling.
Dataset_Size: 30 participants: 20 healthy volunteers + 10 patients
Training_Data_Source: No model trained or fine-tuned in the paper, just used the vendor's model.
Other_Metrics: LVEF, LVEDVi, strain aSNR, aCNR Likert subjective quality Acquisition time reduction
Performance_Summary: DL-reconstructed low-resolution cine reduced acquisition time by 35–42% while maintaining equivalent ventricular function metrics, strain, aSNR/aCNR, and subjective image quality compared with standard cine. Agreement was excellent (e.g., LVEF ICC ≈ 0.98).
Clinical_Results: Volumetry and strain showed no significant differences between cineDL and standard cine; subjective quality remained excellent, supporting preserved diagnostic utility despite faster acquisition.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: Improves scan efficiency and throughput, which can benefit constrained settings, but relies on proprietary vendor DL reconstruction on 3T systems and does not target low-field or low-cost deployment.
Main_Finding_1: DL super-resolution reconstruction enables 35–42% shorter cine acquisition times without degrading cardiac functional measurements.
Main_Finding_2: Volumetric parameters and strain show excellent agreement between DL-reconstructed and standard cine (e.g., LVEF ICC ≈ 0.98).
Main_Finding_3: Subjective image quality, aSNR, and aCNR remain comparable, indicating preserved diagnostic image quality despite lower acquisition resolution.
Limitations_Mentioned: Small cohort; single center; readers could often distinguish reconstructions; dependence on training data; unequal acquisition order; no dedicated assessment of potential information loss or hallucinations.
Notes_Questions: Uses proprietary CNN pipeline with limited transparency

Title: Convolutional Neural Network-based Enhancement Method of 3D MRI
Architecture_Specifics: 3D CNN
Dataset_Size: 48 3D image volumes
Training_Data_Source: BrainWeb simulated database
Other_Metrics: MSE
Performance_Summary: The proposed 3D CNN model achieved PSNR of 37.09 dB and SSIM of 0.9796 for 2x upsampling, outperforming cubic interpolation (36.43 dB). It effectively improved slice-select direction resolution using residual learning.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: Method evaluated on synthetic data only. While CNNs are standard, no discussion of low-resource deployment or low-field MRI
Main_Finding_1: Proposed CNN method achieved superior quantitative results (PSNR: 37.09 dB, SSIM: 0.9796 for 2x scaling) compared to linear and cubic interpolation.
Main_Finding_2: The method successfully generates a residual volume to reconstruct high-resolution MRI with improved slice-select direction resolution.
Main_Finding_3: Qualitative visual inspection shows sharper edges and structural details compared to interpolation methods.
Limitations_Mentioned: Evaluated only on simulated/synthetic data (BrainWeb); no real clinical data validation mentioned.
Notes_Questions: Does the model generalize to real MRI noise/artifacts (bias field, motion) not present in the simulation?

Title: Cerebrovascular super-resolution 4D Flow MRI - Sequential combination of resolution enhancement by deep learning and physics-informed image processing to non-invasively quantify intracranial velocity, flow, and relative pressure
URL: Sequential combination of resolution enhancement by deep learning and physics-informed image processing to non-invasively quantify intracranial velocity, flow, and relative pressure - PubMed
Architecture_Specifics: 4DFlowNet (Deep Residual Network)
Dataset_Size: 4 in-silico models, 8 in-vivo volunteers
Training_Data_Source: Patient-specific CFD models and in-vivo data from 3T scanner
Other_Metrics: RMSE, Relative Error, Cosine Similarity, MAE
Performance_Summary: 4DFlowNet achieved low relative error for velocity (15%) and flow (6.6%) against CFD ground truth. Coupled with vWERP, it recovered relative pressures with 11% error, significantly reducing the bias observed in low-resolution data.
Clinical_Results: uper-resolution reduced pressure estimation error from 23.3% (LR) to 11.0% (SR) relative to CFD. In-vivo, it increased VNR by 3-4x (p<0.001)
Main_Finding_1: Super-resolution combined with vWERP significantly reduced relative pressure estimation error (from 23.3% in Low Res to 11.0% in Super Res) compared to CFD ground truth.
Main_Finding_2: Re-training the network specifically on cerebrovascular data (vs. aortic data) was crucial for accurate pressure recovery.
Main_Finding_3: In-vivo application demonstrated a significant increase in Velocity-to-Noise Ratio (VNR) (3-4x increase).
Limitations_Mentioned: Lack of invasive clinical validation (catheter-based pressure) for in-vivo data; limited number of in-silico training models (n=4).
Notes_Questions: How does the "physics-informed" aspect (vWERP) interact with hallucinations? It seems to act as a constraint/validator.

Title: Deep Learning based Early Detection of Alzheimer’s Disease using Image Enhancement Filters
Architecture_Specifics: Gaussian + SMOTE + CNN
Training_Data_Source: Kaggle Alzheimer's classification dataset.
Other_Metrics: Accuracy 0.92(fi-score)
Performance_Summary: Sampling the images and applying filters influence the accuracy of the CNN model. As evidenced from the results, the dataset that showed the highest overal accuracy of 91.65% combined SMOTE and Gaussian filter.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The standard CNN architecture used in tandem with the SMOTE and Gaussian filters presents adaptability, as this can work on consumer GPU and or CPU offering moderate transferability. Moreover an online website option is a proposed method to represent patient progress.
Main_Finding_1: The overall accuracy of the CNN model improved significantly upon application of SMOTE and Gaussian filters (91.65%) as compared to the CNN method alone (85.73%), and theGaussian used with random sampler and CNN (87.60%).
Main_Finding_2: The use of proper image pre-processing techniques impacts model accuracy and other evaluation metrics.
Main_Finding_3: Balancing the data and using proper filters for image enhancement affects the overall accuracy of early detection models.
Limitations_Mentioned: Not reported in the provided excerpt.
Notes_Questions: SNR was not mentioned. The paper chiefly focuses on the diagnostic applications of CNN used with Gaussian, and SMOTE filters and no information is given regarding the field strength types.

Title: Deep Learning-based Method for Denoising and Image Enhancement in Low-Field MRI
Field_Strength_Type: Low field (64 mT) and Standard field (3 T)
Architecture_Specifics: A modified U-Net architecture was employed for both stage1 and 2. The architecture used in stage 2 comprised of either a single U- Net layer or a combination of two U-Net layers. This approach combined mean squared error (LMSE) and perceptual loss functions (LVGG) as the loss function for the network.
Dataset_Size: 5 datasets containing T1,T2, proton density, diffusion weighted, and dynamic contrast enhanced images from 1.5T or 3T scanners were used
Dataset_Type: PROSTATEx, Prostate-3T, Prostate Fused-MRI-Pathology, Prostate-diagnosis and Prostate MRI.
Other_Metrics: NRMSE (0.633)
Performance_Summary: PSNR, SSIM and NRMSE quantitatively improved image quality though Itirative reconstruction via a two-stage denoising and image enhancing approach trained using a U-Net architecture.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: This approach adresses affordability, compact footprint and reduced shielding requirements.
Main_Finding_1: Model 1.2 (With noise added in both signal and image domain) performed better than Model 1.1 (Noise added in signal domain only), and Model 2.2(Two U-Net layers) performs better than Model 2.1 (one U-Net layer) because predicted images from these models have lower NRMSEs, higher PSNRs, and higher SSIMs values.
Main_Finding_2: Two-stage cascaded implementation outperformed both stage1 and 2 independent implementations.
Main_Finding_3: CNR values for the inference and original images are higher for inference than original image for all four cases, which is concordant with the visual assessment.
Limitations_Mentioned: Low signal-to-noise-ratio

Title: Deep Transfer Learning-Based Multi-Modal Digital Twins for Enhancement and Diagnostic Analysis of Brain MRI Image
Architecture_Specifics: CNN includes the convolution layer and the pooling layer. Four convolution operations are carried out with a convolution kernel size assumed to be 3*3, input image 5*5 and step size 1.
Dataset_Type: Repository of Images of the centra nervous system
Training_Data_Source: Whole Brain Atlas database of Havard Medical School
Other_Metrics: RMSE,RASE, and SAM respectively are (39.2075,116.688 and 0.594)
Performance_Summary: The proposed method achieved a 34.11 PSNR value and 85.24% SSIM value. showing a better capture of higher peak signal to noise ratio with addition of meta-material to the MRI system.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The Digital twins procedure is carried out on an Intel(R) Core(TM) i5, 3.20 GHz CPU with 4GB RAM and all experiments were carried out in Matlab2016a. This set up is cost friendly and is applicable in low-resource settings.
Main_Finding_1: DTs exhibit potential for healthcare to improve service efficiency, throughput and quality, and the patient experience.
Main_Finding_2: The neural network trained in deep learning can be used to transfer medical image diagnosis, crucial in improving accuracy of the doctor's judgement on diseases.
Main_Finding_3: MRI Image quality can be enhanced by adding meta-material to the MRI system which obtains more peak signal-to-noise ratio.
Limitations_Mentioned: The study cites that the MRI image enhancement based on meta-material composite meta-surface proposed in the experiment only verifies the results of taking MRI images in the knee region, and the enhancement effect in other parts of the human body required further verification. Secondly, the medical, The proposed image fusion algorithm takes a long time to run, necessitating further optimization.
Notes_Questions: The data used in the digital twins has to be collected from sensors placed on individuals, which creates a higher level of ambiguity. The method does not clearly show how the Digital twins and the sensor information work in tandem, but it clearly shows how the convolution using the neural network of choice (CNN)operates.

Title: Digital Image Enhancement using Conventional Neural Network
MRI_Application_Area: Digital Image Enhancement using conventional neural network
Architecture_Specifics: Pre-trained convolutional layers are used in the SRCNN architecture to learn higher-level feature representation using a pooling operation.
PSNR_Value: Multiple images are considered from different sources and of varying sizes. For these values represented in the set given, the corresponding SSIM values will be given in a similar set in the row to the right(26.5,31.9,31.4,34.8,31.32,31.66)
Performance_Summary: The SRCNN method exhibits strong capability in the enhancement of low-resolution images.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The work generally proposes the SRCNN architecture as a suitable image enhancement architecture for a myriad of applications in various fields, giving it a broader coverage of applicability.
Main_Finding_1: The SRCNN method learns the mapping from low-resolution to high-resolution images from scratch, with minimal pre- and post-processing for optimization.
Main_Finding_2: Image super resolution technique can be applied in various real-world scenarios.
Main_Finding_3: SRCNN can enhance the quality of low-resolution images
Notes_Questions: The study generally explores the super resolution technique and doesn't provide MRI specific experiments but rather a general architecture (SRCNN) which can be applied with images from any source.

Title: MRI super-resolution using similarity distance and multi-scale receptive field based feature fusion GAN and pre-trained slice interpolation network
Architecture_Specifics: multi-scale receptive field based feature fusion GAN and pre-trained slice interpolation network
Dataset_Size: Approximately 600 MRI scans of healthy persons; 503 patients selected for training and testing.
Training_Data_Source: IXI dataset and BRATS dataset
Other_Metrics: number of training parameters
Performance_Summary: The proposed GAN-based model achieved higher PSNR and SSIM compared to existing MRI super-resolution techniques on IXI and BRATS datasets. The method improved both in-plane and out-of-plane resolution while maintaining lower computational complexity. Visual results showed enhanced structural fidelity and perceptual quality.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The method addresses computational and memory efficiency and uses standard public datasets, making it potentially transferable to resource-limited settings, although it does not explicitly target low-field or LMIC environments.
Main_Finding_1: A multi-slice GAN-based approach leveraging similarity distance and multi-scale receptive fields significantly improves 3D brain MRI super-resolution.
Main_Finding_2: Incorporating a pre-trained slice interpolation network enables effective out-of-plane resolution enhancement with reduced computational cost.
Main_Finding_3: The proposed method outperforms existing CNN- and GAN-based SR techniques on PSNR and SSIM metrics across IXI and BRATS datasets.
Limitations_Mentioned: Evaluation limited to public datasets; no clinical reader study; field strength not specified; performance validated only using image quality metrics.
Notes_Questions: Strong technical validation but lacks clinical evaluation and explicit field strength information.

Title: Pushing the limits of low-cost ultra-low-field MRI by dual-acquisition deep learning 3D superresolution
Architecture_Specifics: Dual-acquisition 3D superresolution model description consisting of 3 modules; the 3D pyramid feature extractor, attentional fusion module, and reconstruction module.
Dataset_Size: 1248 3D T1W and 1182 3D T2W training data from healthy subjects (22–35 years old) sets. 268 and 200 data For T1W or T2W model validation and testing
Training_Data_Source: e WU-Minn Human Connectome Project (HCP S1200), experimental data was obtained from 8 patients recruited from neurology and neurosurgery clinics at Hong Kong Queen Mary Hospital
SSIM_Value: Reported but numerical values not stated
Other_Metrics: Peak SNR
Performance_Summary: The proposed dual-acquisition 3D super-resolution model significantly improved image-quality with enhanced resolution and extremely low level of noise and artifacts for both contrasts and also Numerous fine structural details were successfully recovered with high 3D fidelity using the proposed deep learning model
Clinical_Results: Fine anatomical details of were restored with intrasubject reproducibility, intercontrast consistency, and confirmed by 3T MRI.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The paper explicitly targets ultra-low-field (0.055T) MRI, emphasizing low-cost, shielding-free, portable scanners suitable for point-of-care use and deployment in low- and middle-income countries. Deep learning is used to compensate for hardware limitations.
Main_Finding_1: Dual-acquisition 3D deep learning super-resolution enables high-quality brain MRI at 0.055T with synthetic 1.5-mm isotropic resolution and effective noise and artifact suppression.
Main_Finding_2: Restored anatomical details are reproducible, contrast-consistent (T1W/T2W), and strongly validated against 3T MRI, indicating high structural fidelity rather than hallucination.
Main_Finding_3: The approach demonstrates feasibility of low-cost, portable MRI systems for clinical brain imaging with total scan times under 20 minutes.
Limitations_Mentioned: Use of synthetically degraded high-field data for training; limited quantitative metric reporting; reduced performance in low-SNR regions such as the cerebellum; limited evaluation across diverse pathologies. limitation of human 2D vision, as demonstrated by the difficulty if one attempts to visually relate various 3D structures before and after 3D superresolution
Notes_Questions: Strong qualitative and clinical validation, but lacks explicit PSNR/SSIM numerical reporting. Future work needed for broader pathology-specific evaluation and faster acquisition strategies.

Title: Joint B0B0​ and Image Reconstruction in Low-Field MRI by Physics-Informed Deep-Learning
Primary_Focus: denoising and distortion correction in low‑field MRI
Architecture_Specifics: spherical‑harmonic network for B0 estimation and a U‑Net for image denoising.
Dataset_Size: 1260 training, 360 validation, 180 testing
Training_Data_Source: three-dimensional single-coil spin-echo acquisitions of the knee at 3 T from the FastMRI dataset converted to low‑field MRI
Other_Metrics: RMSE
Performance_Summary: The proposed physics informed Deep Learning method significantly improves image reconstruction quality in low‑field MRI compared to classical model‑based approaches. It increases PSNR by up to 11.7% and reduces RMSE by up to 86.3%
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The paper explicitly develops reconstruction methods for low‑field MRI, which are more affordable and deployable in resource‑limited environments, and discusses implications for use in resource‑poor areas.
Main_Finding_1: Physics‑informed deep learning significantly outperforms classical model‑based reconstruction in low‑field MRI, boosting PSNR and reducing RMSE.
Main_Finding_2: A novel SH‑Net that enforces spherical harmonic field smoothness improves B0 estimation accuracy.
Main_Finding_3: End to end unrolled alternating optimization yields robust image and field map reconstructions from single low‑field MRI acquisitions.
Limitations_Mentioned: Authors note the use of simulated data only and plan future work with real measured low‑field MRI.
Notes_Questions: Dataset is based on simulated low-field MRI from 3T knee data, which may not fully capture real low-field noise, artifacts, or system variability. No clinical evaluation or in-vivo low-field validation was performed, so generalizability to real-world low-field scans is uncertain.

Title: Multi-Contrast Super-Resolution MRI Through a Progressive Network
Architecture_Specifics: Multi-Contrast Super-Resolution MRI Through a Progressive Network
Dataset_Size: IXI dataset: 578 patients; 7,000 T2–PD image pairs from 56 subjects for training and 1,955 pairs from 16 subjects for testing. NAMIC dataset: 20 patients; 1,620 T2–T1 image pairs from 18 subjects for training and 180 pairs from 2 subjects for testing
Training_Data_Source: IXI Dataset, NAMIC Dataset
PSNR_Value: For the IXI dataset, PSNR improved from 27.15 dB in the 4×LR images to 33.33 dB with the non-progressive model, 34.81 dB with the U-PRO model, and reached 35.14 dB with the C-PRO model. For the NAMIC dataset, PSNR increased from 26.83 dB in the 4×LR images to 28.21 dB with the non-progressive model, 30.76 dB with the U-PRO model, and 30.94 dB with the C-PRO model.
SSIM_Value: For the IXI dataset, SSIM increased from 0.793 in the 4×LR images to 0.950 with the non-progressive model, 0.958 with the U-PRO model, and 0.961 with the C-PRO model. For the NAMIC dataset, SSIM improved from 0.751 in the 4×LR images to 0.925 with the non-progressive model, 0.951 with the U-PRO model, and 0.953 with the C-PRO model.
Other_Metrics: NMSE, MSE
Performance_Summary: The proposed progressive multi-contrast network achieved higher PSNR and SSIM than baseline interpolation and single-contrast SR methods. Results demonstrate improved structural fidelity and consistency across MRI contrasts.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: Uses a CNN-based super-resolution approach that is transferable to other MRI systems, but the study does not address low-field imaging or resource-constrained environments.
Main_Finding_1: Progressive multi-contrast learning significantly improves MRI super-resolution performance compared to single-contrast methods.
Main_Finding_2: Joint reconstruction across contrasts preserves structural consistency between MRI modalities.
Main_Finding_3: Progressive training strategy stabilizes learning and improves high-frequency detail recovery
Limitations_Mentioned: Study limited to a specific dataset; lack of clinical validation and evaluation on diverse scanners.
Notes_Questions: MRI field strength is not specified for either dataset. The study reports strong quantitative improvements (PSNR/SSIM) but lacks clinical validation or radiologist evaluation.

Title: FA-GAN: Fused attentive generative adversarial networks for MRI image super-resolution
MRI_Application_Area: Cardiac, brain, knee, multimodal whole heart
Primary_Focus: Generate high resolution from low resolution images
Architecture_Specifics: FA-GAN with Local Fusion Feature Block , Global Feature Fusion Block, channel-attention module, self-attention module, spectral normalization in the discriminator
Dataset_Size: 50 3D MRI volumes total: 40 training (3,200 2D slices) and 10 validation (960 2D slices); additional 10 3D samples used as independent test set (960 2D slices)
Dataset_Type: Cardiac, brain, knee, multimodal whole heart
PSNR_Value: For 4× super-resolution: Cardiac: 34.28 dB; Brain: 42.11 dB; Knee: 30.27 dB; MM-WHS: 36.81 dB
SSIM_Value: For 4× super-resolution: Cardiac: 0.9966; Brain: 0.9974; Knee: 0.9926; MM-WHS: 0.9981
Performance_Summary: FA-GAN outperforms SRGAN, CA-SR-GAN, and SA-SR-GAN in PSNR, SSIM, and FID across multiple MRI datasets and scales (×2, ×4, ×8). It better preserves high-frequency details, textures, and edges and suppresses aliasing artifacts, especially at 4× upsampling
Clinical_Results: No
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: Increase effective resolution without upgrading to more expensive scanners
Main_Finding_1: The fused attention architecture (local feature fusion + global feature fusion with channel and self-attention) significantly improves MRI super-resolution performance compared to standard SRGAN variants
Main_Finding_2: FA-GAN achieves consistently higher PSNR/SSIM and lower FID across cardiac, brain, knee, and MM-WHS datasets at multiple upsampling factors
Limitations_Mentioned: Experiments are limited to relatively small datasets and do not include clinical validation; evaluation focuses on image quality metrics, with no assessment of diagnostic impact or generalization
Data_Quality_Flag: Lack of details about image quality

Title: Deep learning myocardial infarction segmentation framework from cardiac magnetic resonance images
Primary_Focus: Multi-class semantic segmentation of myocardial infarction and microvascular obstruction
Architecture_Specifics: Encoder–decoder architectures
Dataset_Size: 150 patients (100 with ground truth, 50 without ground truth); 708 labeled images used for training/testing
Training_Data_Source: EMIDEC dataset
Other_Metrics: Accuracy, Recall, Precision, F1-score, Mean Intersection over Union (mIoU), FPS, FLOPs
Performance_Summary: Best model (ResU-Net) achieved Acc = 88.48%, F1 = 85.35, mIoU = 84.23 across all classes
Clinical_Validation_Type: Retrospective validation on expert-annotated dataset; qualitative visual validation on unlabeled cases
Clinical_Results: Accurate segmentation of LV, myocardium, MI, and MVO; improved detection of infarct regions compared to baseline architectures
Resource_Constraints_Addressed: discusses computational efficiency, model size, FPS, and preprocessing impact
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: on Uses standard 1.5T MRI and public data
Main_Finding_1: CLAHE preprocessing significantly improves segmentation performance over raw images
Limitations_Mentioned: Single-dataset evaluation; limited external validation; lack of ground truth for part of the dataset
Notes_Questions: Full article not available

Title: ShuffleUNet: Super resolution of diffusion-weighted MRIs using deep learning
Primary_Focus: Single-image super-resolution for 3D diffusion-weighted brain MRI to recover high-resolution volumes from low-resolution inputs, enabling shorter scan times and reducing motion artefacts
Architecture_Specifics: 3D encoder–decoder UNet variant with: 4 downsampling blocks and 4 upsampling blocks. replaces strided convolutions/max-pooling with pixel unshuffle (lossless pooling) for downsampling; replaces deconvolution/interpolation with pixel shuffle (sub-pixel convolution) for upsampling; uses convolutional decomposition (four parallel convolutions per block, used as skip connections); no batch normalization; initial filters = 64, doubled each down step and halved each up step; pixel shuffle and pixel unshuffle layers have learnable parameters; weights initialized with Kaiming Normal
Dataset_Size: 399 subjects from IXI dataset: 300 training, 50 test, 49 validation
Dataset_Type: Public IXI diffusion-weighted MRI of healthy subjects
Training_Data_Source: Diffusion-weighted brain MRI from IXI dataset (public); low-resolution data simulated by FSL under-sampling by factor 2 in all dimensions; then sinc-interpolated back to HR grid for input to UNet-based models.
Performance_Summary: ShuffleUNet outperforms trilinear interpolation, sinc interpolation, and standard UNet on structural DW-MRI SR and on derived diffusion metrics. It achieves higher SSIM and UQI and lower RMSE, and the improvements are statistically significant (p < 0.01).
Clinical_Results: no clinical results
Resource_Constraints_Addressed: Simulates 2× downsampling in each dimension, corresponding to a theoretical 8× acceleration in acquisition, which would reduce scan time and motion artefacts.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: approach is software-based, works on standard 1.5T/3T MRI, and is explicitly aimed at reducing scan time
Main_Finding_1: ShuffleUNet significantly improves single-image super-resolution of DW-MRI compared to trilinear, sinc, and UNet
Limitations_Mentioned: - Low-resolution data are artificially downsampled; no real accelerated acquisitions or prospective low-resolution protocols tested. - Dataset limited to healthy subjects from IXI; no diseased brains, no clinical endpoints. - Only diffusion-weighted MRIs; generalization to other contrasts or sites not evaluated. - Requires GPU training (V100) and DL expertise; code and deployment pathway not discussed in detail.
Data_Quality_Flag: there’s no heterogeneity across scanners/centers or pathological cases

Title: Transfer learning in deep neural network based under-sampled MR image reconstruction
Primary_Focus: Assessing generalization and applying Transfer Learning
Architecture_Specifics: U-net
Dataset_Size: Source (Brain 1.5T): 1407 images. Target (Brain 3T): 500 images. Target (Cardiac): 500 images.
Training_Data_Source: Brain: 1.5T (various patients) and OASIS-3 (3T). Cardiac: Open-source dataset from York University.
PSNR_Value: Brain 3T (500 images): 29.26 dB. Cardiac (500 images): 35.02 dB.
Performance_Summary: Fine-tuning significantly outperformed conventional CG-SENSE and the direct application of the pre-trained network without adjustments.
Clinical_Validation_Type: Quantitative and qualitative comparison (image artifacts) against fully-sampled reference images.
Clinical_Results: Successful reconstructions with reduced aliasing artifacts and preservation of structural edges.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: Proposes methods requiring fewer new data points to adapt AI models, facilitating implementation in centers with lower data volumes.
Main_Finding_1: U-Net generalization capability is limited when there is a change in scanner, anatomy, or acceleration factor.
Main_Finding_2: End-to-end fine-tuning allows knowledge transfer with as few as 50 to 300 new images
Limitations_Mentioned: Study focused only on uniform Cartesian trajectories and the specific technique of fine-tuning.

Title: Enhancement of Image Quality in Low-Field Knee MR Imaging Using Deep Learning
Architecture_Specifics: Fat-suppression contrast: U-Net generates FS-T2WI from T1WI+T2WI Super-resolution: RED-Net encoder–decoder with skip connections; workflow downsamples 3T to mimic low-field then trains to restore HR; inference upsamples low-field to 448×448
Dataset_Size: 3T training: 45 patients with knee disorders + 6 normal subjects (12 studies) 0.4T evaluation: 25 patients with suspected knee disorders
Training_Data_Source: 3T knee MRI (Siemens Magnetom Skyra, 8-channel coil) used to train both models; applied to 0.4T knee MRI (Fujifilm APERTO Lucent).
Other_Metrics: 5-point subjective image-quality scores (anatomy depiction + noise) Abnormal finding counts with McNemar tests (per structure category) Diagnostic confidence (Wilcoxon signed-rank), significant for most categories
Performance_Summary: DL enhancement significantly improves subjective image quality (anatomy depiction and noise) vs original 0.4T images (p<0.001). Abnormality detection increases in some categories (e.g., cartilage for reader 2; muscle/soft tissue for both readers), and diagnostic confidence improves for most structures (p<0.001), except joint effusion.
Clinical_Validation_Type: Multi-reader (2 board-certified radiologists), with statistical testing
Clinical_Results: Significant gains in perceived quality and reduced noise Increased abnormal findings in specific categories (cartilage reader 2 p=0.001; muscle/soft tissue p<0.05) Higher diagnostic confidence for cartilage/meniscus/ligaments/bone marrow/muscle (p<0.001), not joint effusion
Resource_Constraints_Addressed: Yes (explicit focus on low-field as cost-effective)
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: Directly targets low-field MRI, which matters for LMICs, but uses relatively outdated CNN-based SR, lacks objective fidelity/hallucination safeguards, and has limited validation
Main_Finding_1: A U-Net model can synthesize improved fat-suppressed T2-weighted images from low-field knee MRI inputs by leveraging training on 3T data.
Main_Finding_2: A RED-Net super-resolution network enhances low-field (0.4T) knee MRI resolution and reduces noise (256×256→448×448), significantly improving subjective image quality.
Main_Finding_3: Radiologists reported higher diagnostic confidence and detected more abnormalities in some structures (e.g., cartilage, soft tissue) on DL-enhanced images compared to original low-field scans.
Limitations_Mentioned: Small cohort, onld method, sagittal-only evaluation, no paired same-subject 0.4T vs 3T ground truth, no pathological confirmation, and need for larger multi-condition validation.
Notes_Questions: Method relies on relatively outdated CNN-based SR (U-Net + RED-Net) with no comparison to modern diffusion/transformer approaches; no objective fidelity metrics (PSNR/SSIM) or hallucination/safety evaluation, and generalization is limited due to small low-field cohort and lack of paired 0.4T/3T ground truth.

Title: Multisequence 3-T Image Synthesis from 64-mT Low-Field-Strength MRI Using Generative Adversarial Networks in Multiple Sclerosis
Architecture_Specifics: LowGAN (three parallel pix2pix layers with U-Net generator and wavelet-Fourier filter)
Dataset_Size: 50 participants in the main group, 13 participants in the validation group
Training_Data_Source: Hospital of the University of Pennsylvania and National Institutes of Health Clinical Center
Other_Metrics: NCC, FSIM (Feature Similarity Index), Absolute Volume, Dice score
Performance_Summary: LowGAN achieved higher SSIM, PSNR, NCC, and FSIM compared to native 64-mT images across T1w and FLAIR contrasts. It also accurately estimated cerebral cortex and thalamic volumes matching 3-T measurements.
Clinical_Results: LowGAN increased white matter lesion segmentation agreement with 3-T imaging (Dice score, 0.32 vs 0.28; P < .001) pointing toward increased lesion conspicuity
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The paper directly addresses the capital investment barriers in low-resource settings by enhancing images from portable, less expensive 64-mT MRI scanners.
Main_Finding_1: LowGAN significantly improved objective image quality metrics (SSIM, PSNR, NCC, FSIM) for low-field-strength images, particularly for T1-weighted and FLAIR sequences.
Main_Finding_2: The method enhanced morphometric measurements, correcting the underestimation seen in native 64-mT images for major structures like the thalamus.
Main_Finding_3: LowGAN successfully preserved white matter lesions and increased their conspicuity and segmentation Dice scores without artificially hallucinating lesions in healthy controls.
Limitations_Mentioned: Focused on MS patients, relatively small dataset, requires paired high-field and low-field images for training, and cannot generate lesions absent on the original low-field images.
Notes_Questions: Published 2025 version of a previous preprint. Unlike the preprint, this peer-reviewed version includes explicit PSNR and SSIM metrics, a new validation cohort of 13 patients, and FSIM evaluation.

Title: Evaluation of epilepsy lesion visualisation enhancement in low-field MRI using image quality transfer: a preliminary investigation of clinical potential for applications in developing countries
Architecture_Specifics: Anisotropic U-Net
Dataset_Size: 12 patients (5 to 18 years old, 7 males, 5 females).
Training_Data_Source: Training: High-resolution images from public datasets (WU-Minn Human Connectome Project, Leipzig Study for Mind-Body-Emotion Interactions). Evaluation: Single-center data from patients scanned on a 0.36T and a 1.5T scanner in Ibadan and Ilorin, Nigeria.
Other_Metrics: Not reported
Performance_Summary: When viewed independently, IQT-enhanced images improved GM-WM differentiation scores by 26% (T1) and 17% (T2) compared to LF, but FLAIR scores were 12% lower. Lesion definition scores were mixed but improved when images were viewed side-by-side, becoming 3% higher than LF for FLAIR and T1. Radiologists with HF experience rated IQT images higher than those with LF experience, suggesting a learning curve with the new image appearance.
Clinical_Results: IQT generally improved image quality assessments. However, evaluation of pathology on IQT images was affected by the radiologist's familiarity with HF/IQT appearance. Radiologists with HF experience gave higher scores to IQT images than those with LF experience. The differences in scores between IQT and LF were not statistically significant in this small sample.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The paper directly addresses a clinical need in LMICs by developing and testing a method to improve the diagnostic value of widely available low-field MRI scanners for a critical condition (epilepsy). It explicitly discusses the potential for impact in developing countries and uses data from Nigerian patients for evaluation.
Main_Finding_1: IQT can improve the visual quality of low-field MRI by enhancing grey-white matter differentiation and spatial resolution, particularly in non-axial planes, bringing its appearance closer to that of high-field scans.
Main_Finding_2: Radiologists' scoring of IQT-enhanced images was influenced by their prior experience. Those accustomed to high-field MRI rated IQT images higher than those used to low-field MRI, suggesting a need for familiarization/training with enhanced images in clinical practice.
Main_Finding_3: The performance of the IQT model on FLAIR images was poorer than on T1w and T2w, likely because the high-quality training data (HCP) did not include FLAIR images. This highlights the importance of representative training data.
Limitations_Mentioned: Small sample size (12 patients). FLAIR performance was suboptimal due to lack of FLAIR in the training set. Lesion definition improvement was modest, possibly because the model was trained only on healthy brains. Most lesions were overt and visible even on LF, so the benefit for subtle lesions (like focal cortical dysplasia) could not be properly assessed. The thick-slice acquisition (5mm) is challenging for super-resolution algorithms.
Notes_Questions: This is a strong example of a study designed with LMIC relevance in mind from the outset, using local data and considering the practicalities of implementation. The finding about radiologist experience affecting scores is a crucial insight for real-world deployment.

Title: Deep learning-based single image super-resolution for low-field MR brain images
Architecture_Specifics: SRDenseNet (Dense skip connections architecture)
Dataset_Size: 29,059 training image pairs; 10,000 validation pairs; 7,292 test pairs; 190,000 training patches; 2 low-field brain scans from 2 healthy volunteers
Training_Data_Source: NYU fastMRI Initiative database (fastmri.med.nyu.edu) with T1-weighted, T2-weighted and FLAIR images from 1.5T and 3T scanners; Low-field MRI scanner (50 mT) at Leiden University Medical Center
Other_Metrics: MSE loss function; Training time: ~5 hours on Titan X Geforce GPU (12GB); Network has 1,910,689 trainable parameters
Performance_Summary: SRDenseNet significantly outperformed zero-padding k-space in both PSNR (improvement of ~6-8 dB) and SSIM (improvement of ~0.02-0.04). Network yields sharp SR images where most high-frequency components were recovered. Training on noisy LR images provided strong denoising capabilities.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: Paper explicitly addresses low-field MRI for LMICs. Abstract states 'Low-field MRI scanners... give them the potential to make MRI technology more accessible all around the world.' Discusses portable scanners for rural areas in low- and middle-income countries. Scanner developed for pediatric hydrocephalus imaging in developing countries.
Main_Finding_1: Deep learning-based SISR using SRDenseNet can significantly improve low-field MR brain image quality, achieving PSNR improvements of 6-8 dB and SSIM improvements of 0.02-0.04 compared to zero-padding interpolation.
Main_Finding_2: Training on high-field MRI data with added noise successfully transfers to real low-field MRI images, demonstrating effective domain adaptation without requiring large low-field datasets.
Main_Finding_3: The network simultaneously performs denoising and super-resolution, which is particularly relevant for low-field MRI where SNR is inherently lower than high-field systems.
Limitations_Mentioned: 2D only (not 3D); trained on high-field data (potential modeling errors); low-field HR images contain some noise (not objective ground truth); CNNs can produce artifacts or fail to produce significant details; no radiologist evaluation; limited to specific image size (64x64 to 128x128)
Notes_Questions: Excellent LMIC relevance - explicitly designed for low-field MRI accessibility. Uses public fastMRI dataset. Network architecture (SRDenseNet) is well-established. Future work includes 3D extension and transfer learning with low-field data. Funded by NWO-WOTRO and TU Delft Global Initiative for Global Development.

Title: Deep learning for fast low-field MRI acquisitions
Architecture_Specifics: Residual U-net with 2-channel input/output (real and imaginary parts); 4-level encoder-decoder with 3x3 convolutions, ReLU activations, max-pooling, skip connections, and residual shortcut connections; MSE loss; RMSProp optimizer; trained with data augmentation using geometric transformations on complex 3D images
Dataset_Size: 10 fully sampled 3D in vivo wrist/hand scans for training and validation (8 train, 2 validation); 6 additional scans for retrospective testing; 6 separate scans for prospective testing
Training_Data_Source: In-house dataset acquired at 0.1T on a compact biplanar system at the University of Basel (AMT Center); 3D spoiled GRE wrist images; not publicly available but available on request for noncommercial research
PSNR_Value: Magnitude: 29.121 at R=3; 27.698 at R=4; 26.642 at R=5 (U-net vs FT baselines of 28.032, 25.655, 24.424 respectively). Phase: 26.527 at R=3; 25.703 at R=4; 24.855 at R=5
Other_Metrics: NRMSE (magnitude and phase); gradient magnitude (reference-free sharpness metric, magnitude only); Kruskal-Wallis and Wilcoxon non-parametric statistical tests; prospective gradient improvement of 9.4% at R=5
Performance_Summary: Residual U-net significantly outperformed conventional Fourier Transform reconstruction across all acceleration rates (R=3, 4, 5) for both magnitude and phase images (p < 0.01667). Sharpness improved by approximately 13% at R=5. Prospective validation on fivefold undersampled data confirmed results consistent with retrospective findings. Sampling pattern with CL=7 and sigma_y=0.10 gave the best overall trade-off between artifact suppression and detail preservation.
Clinical_Validation_Type: Prospective validation on acquired undersampled data
Clinical_Results: Model trained on only 10 subjects generalized successfully to prospectively acquired fivefold undersampled wrist MRI at 0.1T; global structure and edge sharpness were preserved in both magnitude and phase images; results suggest feasibility of reducing acquisition time from approximately 15 minutes to approximately 3 minutes
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The paper directly targets low-field MRI at 0.1T, explicitly motivated by reduced cost, reduced footprint, and wider global accessibility; addresses the key challenge of small training datasets at LF which is directly relevant to LMIC settings where large curated databases are unavailable; proposes data augmentation as a practical solution to data scarcity; prospective validation on actual LF hardware strengthens clinical translation potential in resource-limited environments
Main_Finding_1: A Residual U-net trained with only 10 subjects and data augmentation can reconstruct undersampled 0.1T MRI with statistically significant improvements in PSNR, SSIM, NRMSE, and sharpness over standard Fourier Transform reconstruction across three to fivefold acceleration rates, demonstrating viability with minimal training data
