Main_Finding_2: Both magnitude and phase images were reconstructed with comparable performance, which is notable since most prior DL MRI reconstruction studies focused solely on magnitude; this opens potential for phase-contrast MRI applications such as susceptibility mapping and flow imaging at low field
Main_Finding_3: Prospective validation on actually acquired fivefold undersampled LF data confirmed performance consistent with the retrospective simulation study, a key step toward clinical translation that is often absent in DL MRI reconstruction literature
Limitations_Mentioned: Small and homogeneous training dataset limits generalization; remaining image smoothness due to MSE loss function and high-frequency loss from undersampling; model performance is highly dependent on the chosen sampling pattern and could not correct local artifacts inside ROIs for some patterns; training restricted to a single sequence type and single anatomy; no pathological cases tested; parallel imaging not applicable at this field strength
Code_Available: No (data available on request; no code repository mentioned)
Notes_Questions: This paper is one of the few to directly train and validate a DL reconstruction model on real low-field data rather than simulated LF data from high-field databases; the prospective validation is a key differentiator from most comparable work; training computation was done on a single GTX 1080 Ti GPU in 1.5 hours, suggesting low compute requirements suitable for resource-constrained settings; no clinical pathology was included in the dataset which is an important gap for real-world deployment

Title: Deep Learning Super-Resolution Reconstruction for Fast and Motion-Robust T2-weighted Prostate MRI
Primary_Focus: Super-resolution (SR) and denoising to reduce acquisition time while maintaining or improving image quality in T2-weighted prostate MRI
AI_Architecture: Deep Learning (DL) framework integrating compressed sensing with convolutional neural networks
Architecture_Specifics: Combines two networks: Adaptive-CS-Net (for sparsity-constrained reconstruction and denoising) and Precise-Image-Net (for ringing artifact removal and resolution upscaling)
Dataset_Size: 109 male participants
Dataset_Type: Prospective study of adult male patients with suspected prostate cancer
Training_Data_Source: Precise-Image-Net was trained on 6 million image pairs of originally high-resolution and secondary downscaled images
PSNR_Value: Not explicitly reported in the provided text (qualitative Likert scales and quantitative "Edge Rise Distance" were used instead)
Other_Metrics: Edge Rise Distance (ERD), apparent SNR, apparent CNR, and acquisition time
Clinical_Validation_Type: PI-RADS score agreement assessment by two board-certified radiologists with intra- and interreader reproducibility testing
Clinical_Results: Excellent agreement for PI-RADS scores (Cohen $\kappa$ 0.92–0.94); 78–86% of score shifts moved from PI-RADS 3 to either 2 or 4
Resource_Constraints_Addressed: Addressed via reduced scan time (accelerated acquisition)
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: While the study was conducted on high-end 3.0 T hardware not always available in LMICs, its core focus is on resource optimization through drastic reductions in MRI acquisition time. By using Deep Learning (DL) to reconstruct low-resolution data into high-quality images, the method reduced scan times by 29% to 36%. In LMIC settings, such acceleration can significantly increase patient throughput, reduce the "cost per scan," and make high-quality prostate cancer screening more feasible with limited machine availability. Additionally, the improved motion-robustness of the DL sequence reduces the need for repeated scans, which is a critical benefit in busy clinical environments with limited resources.
Main_Finding_1: DL reconstruction enables significantly faster T2-weighted prostate MRI acquisition times (164s vs. 257s/230s)
Main_Finding_2: DL reconstruction significantly improves qualitative and quantitative image sharpness compared to standard sequences
Main_Finding_3: There is excellent clinical agreement between DL-reconstructed and standard sequences for PI-RADS ratings
Limitations_Mentioned: Only evaluated T2-weighted sequences and was an exploratory single-center study

Title: Super resolution using sparse sampling at portable ultra-low field MR
Primary_Focus: Developing a Fourier-based Super Resolution (FouSR) approach to enhance ULF MRI resolution with minimal scan time increase
AI_Architecture: Non-AI / Fourier-based (Specifically stated as NOT relying on deep learning)
Architecture_Specifics: Combines spatial frequencies from two orthogonal ULF images; replaces the inner k-space core of one plane with the core from another
Dataset_Size: 10 participants (6 female) plus a phantom
Dataset_Type: Patients with MS or suspected MS
Other_Metrics: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR), Contrast-to-Noise Ratio (CNR), Lesion Conspicuity, and Image Sharpness (Laplacian variance)
Performance_Summary: FouSR demonstrated superior image sharpness ($0.025 \pm 0.0040$) compared to standard ULF scans and average methods
Clinical_Validation_Type: Blinded qualitative assessment by three experienced MS neurologists using a defined Likert scale
Clinical_Results: No significant difference in visualization of white matter lesions or sulci/gyri compared to other methods, though sharpness was improved
Resource_Constraints_Addressed: Addressed cost-effectiveness, reduced power, and portability for accessible MRI
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The paper highlights that traditional high-field MRI scanners face substantial financial barriers regarding purchase, installation, and maintenance, which restricts access in developing and underdeveloped countries. The study's focus on portable ultra-low field (0.064 T) MRI is directly aimed at increasing global accessibility due to its cost-effectiveness, reduced power requirements, and portability. By developing a "FouSR" method that improves image sharpness without requiring large datasets or high computational power (unlike deep learning), the research provides a scalable solution for improving diagnostic quality in resource-constrained settings.
Main_Finding_1: FouSR recovers information from under-sampled directions to create isotropic images with significantly higher sharpness
Main_Finding_2: The method can be implemented on the scanner with an estimated 60% increase in scan time compared to a single plane
Main_Finding_3: Unlike deep learning methods, FouSR does not require synthetic data or large training sets, enhancing generalizability
Limitations_Mentioned: Potential introduction of ringing artifacts and dependency on high-quality image registration
Data_Quality_Flag: Acceptable (Clinical cohort and standard phantom validation used)

Title: Bridging the gap: improving correspondence between low-field and high-field magnetic resonance images in young people
Field_Strength_Type: Ultra-low-field (64 mT / 0.064 T) vs. High-field (3.0 T)
Primary_Focus: Testing super-resolution methods to improve the quality and correspondence of low-field MRI scans with high-field standards in young people
Architecture_Specifics: Used SynthSR (v. 1.0), a CNN that predicts 1 mm isotropic 3D MP-RAGE images from low-resolution inputs
Dataset_Size: 70 individuals (Final sample)
Dataset_Type: Community sample of young people (Ages 9–26 years)
Training_Data_Source: SynthSR was used (it is typically trained on synthetic data, though the paper uses a "Hyperfine-specific version")
Other_Metrics: Pearson’s r, ICC, Steiger Z-tests, and Bland-Altman agreement
Performance_Summary: SynthSR significantly improved correspondence for total brain volume (r=0.97$), white matter volume (r=0.92$), and subcortical volume (r=0.88$)
Clinical_Validation_Type: Quantitative comparison of automated brain segmentation (FreeSurfer) measures between low-field and high-field scans
Clinical_Results: High correspondence for global volumes (intracranial, total brain); however, finer measures like cortical thickness remain challenging (r=0.14$ with SynthSR)
Resource_Constraints_Addressed: Addressed the potential for scaling MR technology in low-resource and community settings
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The paper explicitly notes the potential of portable low-field systems to make MR technology available "at scale in low-resource settings" due to lower costs, reduced power, and lack of cryogens.
Main_Finding_1: Standard single-acquisition low-field scans show moderate-to-high correspondence with 3T for global volumes but poor correspondence for white matter and cortical thickness.
Main_Finding_2: SynthSR-based super-resolution significantly improves the fidelity of low-field scans to high-field standards across most global and regional measures.
Main_Finding_3: Multi-orientation image averaging also improves correspondence for specific measures like white matter, but SynthSR generally provided broader improvements.
Limitations_Mentioned: Finer-scale measurements (cortical thickness) still lack accuracy; total scan duration for multiple orientations was long (52.25 min).
Data_Quality_Flag: Acceptable (Validated via MRIQC and radiologist review)

Title: MRI Super-Resolution With Ensemble Learning and Complementary Priors
Architecture_Specifics: WGAN-GP with modified encoder-decoder generator (originally from CT denoising), ensemble of 5 GANs + 1 ensemble GAN
Dataset_Size: 159 patient scans with 5,744 slices for training/validation (80%/20%), 43 patient scans with 1,500 slices for testing
Training_Data_Source: Public fastMRI knee dataset from NYU (single-coil acquisition, proton density weighted)
Other_Metrics: Not reported
Performance_Summary: The proposed ensemble learning method achieved PSNR of 36.060 dB and SSIM of 0.920, outperforming individual GAN models (best single: A+ with 34.849 dB PSNR, 0.898 SSIM) and state-of-the-art methods including FSRCNN (28.877 dB), LapSRN (28.804 dB), ESPCN (24.933 dB), and VDSR (28.997 dB). Visual quality assessment showed improved sharpness, reduced artifacts, and more faithful detail preservation compared to competing methods.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: Method was developed and tested exclusively on 1.5T/3T data from fastMRI dataset. The ensemble approach requires training 6 separate GAN models (5 individual + 1 ensemble), increasing computational requirements. No experiments on low-field data or discussion of applicability to resource-limited settings. However, the inference could potentially run on modest hardware once trained.
Main_Finding_1: Ensemble learning of multiple GAN outputs with complementary priors achieves superior MRI super-resolution (PSNR 36.06 dB, SSIM 0.920) compared to any single GAN model or state-of-the-art methods, with 7+ dB improvement over best competing method.
Main_Finding_2: Using different SR algorithms (ZIP, BI, NEDI, SC, A+) to pre-process LR images creates complementary priors that, when combined through ensemble learning, correct individual GAN artifacts and preserve more faithful image details.
Limitations_Mentioned: 1) Only magnitude images used, phase information ignored. 2) Single down-sampling method (k-space truncation keeping central 25%). 3) Only 2x resolution enhancement factor tested. 4) No clinical validation performed. 5) Computational cost of training multiple GANs not fully addressed.
Notes_Questions: 1) Paper published in 2020 - methods may be outdated compared to current transformer/diffusion approaches. 2) Only 2x SR factor tested - unclear if method scales to 4x or higher. 3) Interesting ensemble concept but computational overhead of training 6 GANs is significant. 4) No clinical validation limits immediate clinical applicability.

Title: SRflow: Deep learning based super-resolution of 4D-flow MRI data
Architecture_Specifics: SRflow: Modified WDSR-3D with Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) blocks. Wide activation residual blocks with weight normalization. N=4 residual groups, M=2 residual blocks each. Channel expansion p=32, SE reduction r=8. Voxel shuffling (3D pixel shuffling) for upsampling. Novel 'mutually projected ℓ1' (mp-ℓ1) loss function for direction-sensitive velocity field learning. Combined loss: Jopt = λℓ1·Jℓ1 + λmp-ℓ1·Jmp-ℓ1. Adam optimizer, lr=10^-3, 200 epochs, batch size 4.
Dataset_Size: Synthetic cerebrovascular: 6 patient-specific CFD simulations (120 training + 24 validation samples per 3-fold CV). In vivo cardiovascular: 21 healthy subjects (10 whole heart + 11 thoracic aorta). In vivo cerebrovascular: 3 patients with ICA aneurysm. Total: 24 in vivo + 6 synthetic subjects.
Training_Data_Source: Multi-center. Synthetic: CFD simulations (OpenFOAM) from patient-specific geometries segmented from 3D rotational angiograms. In vivo cardiovascular: 1.5T Siemens Avanto, healthy subjects, prospective ECG triggering. In vivo cerebrovascular: 3T Philips Achieva TX, ICA aneurysm patients, peripheral pulse triggering. Institutions: TUM, University of Zurich, LMU Munich.
PSNR_Value: Not reported (uses PVNR - Peak Velocity-to-Noise Ratio instead). PVNR for 4x SR: Synthetic ~33-34 dB; In vivo cardiovascular ~28-30 dB; In vivo cerebrovascular ~28-30 dB. SRflow(opt) achieves ~1-2 dB improvement over WDSR-3D baseline.
SSIM_Value: Not reported. Paper uses flow-specific metrics: PVNR, RMSspeed, Edir, RMSdiv instead of standard image quality metrics.
Other_Metrics: PVNR (Peak Velocity-to-Noise Ratio): primary metric. RMSspeed: magnitude error (~0.016-0.029 m/s). Edir: direction error (~0.005-0.024). RMSdiv: divergence error (~0.001-0.008 s^-1). Streamline continuity: SRflow(opt) lowest relative error (0.11-0.31 vs 0.66-0.76 for cubic spline). Bland-Altman analysis shows excellent agreement.
Performance_Summary: SRflow(opt) improves PVNR by 10% (cardiovascular) and 30% (cerebrovascular) for 4x SR over cubic B-spline. Statistically significant improvements (p<0.001) for PVNR, RMSspeed, and Edir vs baseline WDSR-3D. Runtime: 10x faster than cubic spline (2.86s vs 73s for 4x SR). Transfer learning from cardiovascular to cerebrovascular data works without fine-tuning. SE blocks and combined mp-ℓ1+ℓ1 loss both contribute to improvements.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: Method offers 10x faster inference than cubic spline (2.86s vs 73s), potentially enabling real-time clinical use. However: requires GPU for training (Quadro P6000), tested only at 1.5T/3T standard-field scanners, datasets not publicly available. Transferable architecture but no low-field validation.
Main_Finding_1: SRflow with combined ℓ1 + mp-ℓ1 loss achieves 10-30% PVNR improvement over cubic B-spline for 4x super-resolution of 4D-flow MRI, with 10x faster inference time enabling potential real-time clinical application.
Main_Finding_2: The novel mutually projected ℓ1 (mp-ℓ1) loss function provides direction-sensitive learning crucial for velocity vector fields. Combined with standard ℓ1, it achieves statistically significant improvements in both magnitude and direction accuracy.
Main_Finding_3: Transfer learning is effective: model trained on in vivo cardiovascular data transfers to cerebrovascular data (different scanner, anatomy, venc) without fine-tuning, demonstrating generalizability across acquisition configurations.
Limitations_Mentioned: Authors identify: (1) Accuracy limited to finite difference schemes and max SR factor from training; (2) Only spatial SR explored, temporal SR is future work; (3) Does not include scan-rescan variability, phase aliasing, eddy current effects; (4) Limited in vivo sample size; (5) No WSS/KE quantitative assessment; (6) Datasets not publicly available.
Notes_Questions: Strong methodological paper with novel contributions: (1) Direction-sensitive loss function for vector fields; (2) SE blocks for 3D flow SR; (3) Extensive ablation studies. First deep learning SR specifically designed for 4D-flow MRI velocity vectors (not just magnitude). Clinical relevance: improved hemodynamic quantification (WSS, vortex cores) depends on accurate velocity fields. PRIMARY RESEARCH - include in systematic review.

Title: SMORE: A Self-Supervised Anti-Aliasing and Super-Resolution Algorithm for MRI Using Deep Learning
Architecture_Specifics: Modified EDSR (Enhanced Deep Residual Networks): ResNet-based without batch normalization, residual scaling, no pooling. Upsampling layer removed for non-integer ratios. Uses 32×32 patches, L1 loss, Adam optimizer (lr=10^-4). Two variants: SMORE(3D) and SMORE(2D) with SAA (Self-supervised Anti-Aliasing) network.
Dataset_Size: 14 T2-weighted brain images (MS patients) for simulation experiments; 33 T2 FLAIR brain images (acquired LR, no ground truth); 1 marmoset PD image.
Training_Data_Source: Self-supervised: No external training data. Training pairs generated from the input image itself. Human data from Johns Hopkins/NIH (MS patients, 3T Philips Achieva).
PSNR_Value: 3D protocol (r=6): SMORE ~29 dB vs sincInterp ~28 dB, LRTV ~24 dB, JogSSR ~27 dB. 2D protocol (r=6): SMORE ~24 dB vs BSP ~22 dB, JogSSR ~23 dB (Figs. 2b, 5b).
SSIM_Value: 3D protocol (r=6): SMORE ~0.75 vs sincInterp ~0.50, LRTV ~0.48, JogSSR ~0.60. 2D protocol (r=6): SMORE ~0.52 vs BSP ~0.45, JogSSR ~0.48 (Figs. 2a, 5a).
Other_Metrics: S3 (Spectral and Spatial Sharpness): original 0.5361 → SMORE 0.5482 (p<0.01). MSE computed. Noise robustness tested: robust to 10-30% Rician noise, fails at 50%.
Performance_Summary: SMORE significantly outperforms all competing methods (LRTV, JogSSR, interpolation) across resolution ratios r=2-6 for both 2D and 3D protocols (p<0.001, Wilcoxon signed rank test). Key: no external training data needed, handles non-integer ratios up to 6.67, preserves MS lesions.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: Self-supervised approach eliminates need for large training datasets. Training ~10 min (pre-trained) to ~40 min (scratch) on Tesla K80 GPU. Enables thicker slice acquisition for faster scans. However: GPU required, tested only at 3T, code upon request only.
Main_Finding_1: SMORE's self-supervised approach learns LR→HR mapping from the image's own HR in-plane slices, eliminating need for external training data. Applicable to any MRI contrast without retraining.
Main_Finding_2: First SSR method to distinguish 2D vs 3D MRI acquisition physics. SMORE(2D) with SAA network removes aliasing artifacts unique to 2D protocols with thick slice separation.
Main_Finding_3: Acquiring thicker slices (r=3) with SMORE achieves quality comparable to thinner slices (r=2) with interpolation, suggesting potential for reduced scan times.
Limitations_Mentioned: Authors identify: (1) Not robust to motion artifacts; (2) Not robust to slice-to-slice gain irregularities; (3) SMORE(2D) not tested with slice separation ≠ slice thickness; (4) No task-specific validation (segmentation); (5) No human observer studies.
Notes_Questions: Strong methodological paper from IEEE TMI. Key innovation: self-supervised learning with distinct modeling for 2D vs 3D acquisition physics. MS lesion preservation visually demonstrated. PRIMARY RESEARCH - include in systematic review. Potential for LMIC adaptation if optimized for lower compute/low-field.

Title: SMIR: A Transformer-Based Model for MRI super-resolution reconstruction
Architecture_Specifics: SMIR: Swin Transformer-based MRI Reconstruction. Two modules: (1) Multi-level feature extraction with K Residual Swin Transformer Blocks (RSTB), each containing S Swin Transformer Layers (STL) + convolutional layer + residual connection; (2) Image reconstruction module. Uses shifted window mechanism for local attention with cross-window communication. Multi-head self-attention (MSA) with learnable relative positional encoding. Combined loss function: L = λ1·L1(spatial) + λ2·L1(frequency domain). Hardware: 4× GTX 1080Ti GPUs.
Dataset_Size: 100 subjects from HCP dataset, 40 slices per subject = 4000 images total. Image size: 256×256 pixels. T1-weighted data. Test set: separate subject with 5 different sampling ratios.
Training_Data_Source: Human Connectome Project (HCP) dataset. Scanner: customized Siemens 3T 'Connectome Skyra' at Washington University in St. Louis. Standard 32-channel Siemens receive head coil. Radial-based k-space undersampling (10%, 20%, 30%, 40%, 50%) to simulate accelerated acquisition.
PSNR_Value: Not reported. Paper uses MAE instead of PSNR.
SSIM_Value: SMIR achieves best SSIM across all sampling ratios: 10%: 0.72; 20%: 0.86; 30%: 0.87; 40%: 0.89; 50%: 0.91. Compared to Real-ESRGAN (0.65-0.78), Rec-DNCNN (0.55-0.86), Gaussian (0.56-0.88), Bicubic (0.54-0.87). (Table I)
Other_Metrics: MAE (Mean Absolute Error): SMIR achieves lowest MAE - 10%: 86.44; 20%: 39.8; 30%: 29.00; 40%: 25.13; 50%: 25.82. Compared to Real-ESRGAN (48.86-106.37), other methods have MAE >130-200.
Performance_Summary: SMIR achieves best SSIM and lowest MAE across all 5 k-space sampling ratios (10-50%) compared to bicubic interpolation, Gaussian filtering, Rec-DNCNN, and Real-ESRGAN. At 20% sampling, SMIR (SSIM=0.86, MAE=39.8) significantly outperforms Real-ESRGAN (SSIM=0.67, MAE=59.75). Visual results show smoother images with preserved details and reduced artifacts.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: Method addresses accelerated MRI (faster scan times via k-space undersampling), which is clinically relevant. Uses public HCP dataset. However: requires 4× GTX 1080Ti GPUs for training, tested only at 3T, code not publicly available. Transformer architecture is computationally intensive. Transferable concept but resource-demanding implementation.
Main_Finding_1: First application of Swin Transformer to MRI reconstruction, combining advantages of CNN (inductive bias, translational equivariance) and Transformer (global context, self-attention) through RSTB blocks with convolutional layers.
Main_Finding_2: Combined spatial and frequency domain loss function improves reconstruction quality. Multi-level feature aggregation from shallow (low-frequency) to deep (high-frequency) layers via residual connections provides rich information for reconstruction.
Main_Finding_3: SMIR outperforms Real-ESRGAN (state-of-the-art for natural images) on medical MRI reconstruction, demonstrating that domain-specific architectures matter. At 20% sampling ratio, achieves SSIM improvement of 0.19 over Real-ESRGAN.
Limitations_Mentioned: Authors acknowledge: (1) Dataset only contains brain region images - need to test on other body parts to verify generalizability; (2) Model trained for specific sampling ratios - real-world sampling rates may vary; (3) No validation on other MRI sequences beyond T1.
Notes_Questions: Conference paper (IEEE ICMIPE 2021) - shorter than journal articles. Novel application of Swin Transformer to MRI. Uses realistic k-space undersampling (radial trajectory) rather than simple image downsampling. Concerns: small test set (1 subject), no statistical testing, no comparison with other MRI-specific SR methods (e.g., SMORE). PRIMARY RESEARCH - include in systematic review. First Transformer-based MRI SR paper in extraction set.

Title: Autoencoder-Inspired Convolutional Network-Based Super-Resolution Method in MRI
Architecture_Specifics: ACNS (Autoencoder-inspired Convolutional Network-based Super-resolution)
Dataset_Size: 4 volunteers; 200 XCAT phantom images; 91 non-medical images; 50 XCAT images; 42 MRIs for training
Training_Data_Source: ViewRay 0.35T MRIgRT at Washington University in St. Louis; 4D XCAT Phantom; 91 non-medical images from Yang et al.
Other_Metrics: IFC (Information Fidelity Criterion): 2.50-3.85; MSE used as loss function; Computation time: 0.15s per 100x100 pixels
Performance_Summary: ACNS achieved comparable PSNR, SSIM, and IFC to SRCNN, FSRCNN, and DRCN. Computation speed was 6x faster than SRCNN, 4x faster than FSRCNN, and 35x faster than DRCN. Average computation time was 0.15s per 100x100 pixels.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: Method explicitly addresses computational efficiency for real-time processing. Uses 0.35T low-field MRI. Network has only 5,728 parameters vs 1,774,080 for DRCN. Could run on modest hardware with fast inference (0.15s per 100x100 pixels). However, requires GPU for training.
Main_Finding_1: ACNS achieved comparable image quality (PSNR, SSIM, IFC) to state-of-the-art methods while being 6-35x faster, with computation time of 0.15s per 100x100 pixels enabling potential real-time 4D MRI applications.
Main_Finding_2: The autoencoder-inspired architecture with fewer parameters (5,728 vs 1,774,080 for DRCN) successfully balances image quality and computational efficiency without sacrificing performance.
Main_Finding_3: Network depth ('the deeper the better') is not always true - more layers and filters did not necessarily improve image quality but increased computation time. Optimal performance requires empirical parameter selection.
Limitations_Mentioned: Small dataset (only 4 volunteers); single-center study; synthetic LR images used (bicubic downsampling); no clinical validation; ACNS accentuated artifacts in some cases; in-plane resolution only (no through-plane); deep learning methods are not transparent.
Notes_Questions: Paper is well-structured with clear methodology. Uses 0.35T MRI which is technically low-field. Authors note future work will address through-plane resolution. IRB approval obtained (HM20004559).

Title: A deep convolutional neural network based computer aided diagnosis system for the prediction of Alzheimer's disease in MRI images
Architecture_Specifics: Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN)
Dataset_Size: Not reported
Training_Data_Source: Public Kaggle MRI dataset
Other_Metrics: MSE: 0.0938, Accuracy: 96%, Precision: 97%, AUC: 98%,
Performance_Summary: The proposed CAD system showed strong performance in both image enhancement and disease classification. Using the 2D Adaptive Bilateral Filter (2D ABF), it achieved a high PSNR of 47.3989 dB and a low MSE of 0.0938, outperforming traditional filters. For Alzheimer’s disease prediction, the DCNN classifier reached 96% accuracy and 97% precision, surpassing the KNN method. The segmentation step, based on Modified Expectation Maximization, achieved a high Dice Coefficient of 0.9389, while the DCNN also produced lower MSE and significantly reduced classification delay compared to KNN.
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: It directly offers a computationally efficient alternative to the existing "financially high costly diagnosis system". The method is characterized as being "fast to deploy" and effective in clinics "only with limited data set for learning"
Main_Finding_1: The Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) classification technique achieved high diagnostic performance for Alzheimer’s disease (AD) prediction, recording an overall classification accuracy of 96% and precision of 97%. This performance was significantly better than the comparative K-nearest neighbor (KNN) algorithm, and the overall DCNN system resulted in a high Area Under Curve (AUC) value of 98%
Main_Finding_2: utilized the 2D Adaptive Bilateral Filter (2D ABF) to improve image quality, yielding a superior Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) of 47.3989 dB and a low Mean Squared Error (MSE) of 0.0938, outperforming standard filters like the 2D Bilateral Filter. Additionally, the segmentation using the Modified Expectation Maximization (MEM) Clustering method achieved a maximum Dice Coefficient of 0.9389
Main_Finding_3: The proposed DCNN methodology demonstrates high computational efficiency for clinical application, showing that the classification delay is considerably decreased compared to the KNN classifier. The solution is characterized as an innovative and efficient method of Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) in clinics that can function effectively "only with limited data set for learning.
Limitations_Mentioned: Not include a dedicated limitations or discussion section
Notes_Questions: Missing Dataset Size

Title: A Novel Deep Learning Approach for Brain Tumors Classification Using MRI Images
Architecture_Specifics: lightweight convolutional neural network (LWCNN)
Dataset_Size: 3160 images
Training_Data_Source: Public Kaggle dataset which is a combination of the following from three datasets; figshare, SARTAJ and Br35H datasets
Other_Metrics: Accuracy: 96.75%
Performance_Summary: The proposed hybrid Lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (LWCNN) framework achieved a high overall classification Accuracy of 96.75% for brain tumor classification (glioma, meningioma, pituitary tumor, and no_tumor). The framework's success is attributed to the image enhancement preprocessing step, which resulted in F-measure scores ranging from 91% to 99% for individual tumor classes. When the image enhancement step was disabled, the F-measure scores dropped significantly (by 4 to 9 percentage points), proving that the enhancement process is critical for improving prediction performance
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The LWCNN is specifically highlighted as "well-suited for deployment on mobile devices and other resource-constrained platforms" due to its low number of parameters and small memory footprint
Main_Finding_1: Image Enhancement is Critical for High Classification Accuracy The inclusion of the image enhancement preprocessing step (unsharp filter and histogram equalization) is critical for achieving high performance, enabling the model to achieve an overall classification accuracy of 96.75%. Without enhancement, the F-measure scores dropped significantly across all four classes (e.g., from 93% to 84% for 'no_tumor')
Main_Finding_2: The Lightweight Model is Highly Effective and Computationally Efficient The proposed Lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (LWCNN) achieved superior efficiency compared to existing state-of-the-art methods, obtaining a classification accuracy of 96.75%. The architecture is designed to be computationally efficient with a low number of parameters, making it well-suited for deployment on resource-constrained platforms
Main_Finding_3: Enhancement Aligns Data Distributions, Improving Generalization The image enhancement process helps align the distributions of image samples between the training dataset and the testing (validation) set, which is crucial for improving the deep learning model's performance on unseen data. This process also resulted in predictions that caused less confusion across different classes compared to using non-enhanced images
Limitations_Mentioned: Not include a dedicated limitations or discussion section
Notes_Questions: A limitation is the absence of clinical validation in the sources, despite the authors stating the proposed approach "holds great promise to be applied in clinical applications

Title: Automatic myocardial segmentation in dynamic contrast enhanced perfusion MRI using Monte Carlo dropout in an encoder-decoder convolutional neural network
Architecture_Specifics: Encoder-decoder convolutional neural network utilizing Monte Carlo dropout
Dataset_Size: 17683 cardiac short-axis
Training_Data_Source: The data used for model development came from a single-center internal dataset collected at Samsung Medical Center, Sungkyunkwan University School of Medicine, Seoul, Republic of Korea, combined with publicly available work. Specifically, the total number of subjects used for model development was 110, which included internal data (n=77) and publicly available work data (n=33)
Other_Metrics: Dice: 0.806
Performance_Summary: The proposed automatic segmentation method achieved a mean Dice similarity score of 0.806 (±0.096) across 35 subjects, which was comparable to the semi-automatic U-Net method's score of 0.808 (±0.084). The automatic method demonstrated a statistically significant correlation with the semi-automatic method (Intraclass Correlation Coefficient = 0.61, P<0.001). Furthermore, the study showed that samples with high segmentation accuracy (Dice score ≥0.85) corresponded to low uncertainty estimates, specifically endocardial Sum of Standard Deviation (SSD) <30 and epicardial SSD <60
LMIC_Relevance_Justification: The method is resource-intensive because it requires 1.5T MRI data, heavy computation during inference (100 Monte Carlo runs per image), and a large training dataset. Even with uncertainty-based screening, it still depends on expensive hardware and significant computational power.
Main_Finding_1: The proposed automatic myocardial segmentation method, which uses uncertainty estimation, achieved a mean Dice similarity score of 0.806 (±0.096), a result comparable to the 0.808 (±0.084) score achieved by the semi-automatic U-Net segmentation method. The fully automatic method also demonstrated a statistically significant correlation with the semi-automatic method (Intraclass Correlation Coefficient = 0.61, P<0.001)
Main_Finding_2: The Monte Carlo dropout uncertainty estimate, calculated as the Sum of Standard Deviation (SSD), successfully guided the selection of the optimal image frame for endocardial and epicardial segmentation. The results showed a strong relationship between accuracy and uncertainty, as highly accurate segmentations (Dice score ≥0.85) corresponded to low uncertainty estimates (endocardial SSD <30 and epicardial SSD <60)
Main_Finding_3: The study demonstrated the feasibility of transferring a U-Net model trained exclusively on cardiac cine data to automatically segment dynamic cardiac perfusion data, a process that did not require manual labeling of the perfusion image data for model development. Furthermore, the SSD metric can be used for screening purposes to identify cases with high uncertainty that require further evaluation and correction by human experts
Limitations_Mentioned: Lower segmentation accuracy, Small patient set for evaluation, High Computational Cost
